coredata.dc:description: Electrolyte leakage from a battery cell can lead to the formation of a dangerous atmosphere in poorly ventilated and enclosed environments due to the high volatility, flammability and toxicity of common electrolyte solvents like dimethyl carbonate (DMC) and diethyl carbonate (DEC). To limit human exposure, the design of detection and warning systems is necessary. To this end, a computational fluid dynamics approach was used to create a vaporisation model to predict the evaporation rate of DMC and DEC, supported by experimental data of the evaporating solvents. All experiments were performed in an enclosed environment with no induced flow. The evaporation flux of DMC and DEC in a fully convective and diffusive environment was 0.70 and 0.15 mg/(cm2 min) at room temperature, respectively. An LES-CFD vaporisation model with a novel liquid-vapour modelling approach was used for the simulation of the evaporated liquids, demonstrating good agreement with the experimental data. The presented model iterated on previous efforts of simulating the evaporation of liquids by accounting for the changes of the evaporation rate brought on by temperature and vapour accumulation around the source, which was done by coupling evaporation, temperature and dispersion modelling dynamically. Results show that DMC was significantly affected by the changes in liquid temperature during the evaporation, highlighting the importance on modelling the local energy balance of the evaporating solvent. The dispersion model showcased good qualitative agreement with the behaviour of heavy gasses, underlining that most of the dispersion of the vapours generated by DMC and DEC is buoyancy-based.

head.ce:title: Li-ion battery electrolyte vaporisation model: An experimental and computational fluid dynamics approach

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Electrolyte leakage from a battery cell can lead to the formation of a dangerous atmosphere in poorly ventilated and enclosed environments due to the high volatility, flammability and toxicity of common electrolyte solvents like dimethyl carbonate (DMC) and diethyl carbonate (DEC). To limit human exposure, the design of detection and warning systems is necessary. To this end, a computational fluid dynamics approach was used to create a vaporisation model to predict the evaporation rate of DMC and DEC, supported by experimental data of the evaporating solvents. All experiments were performed in an enclosed environment with no induced flow. The evaporation flux of DMC and DEC in a fully convective and diffusive environment was 0.70 and 0.15 mg/(cm2 min) at room temperature, respectively. An LES-CFD vaporisation model with a novel liquid-vapour modelling approach was used for the simulation of the evaporated liquids, demonstrating good agreement with the experimental data. The presented model iterated on previous efforts of simulating the evaporation of liquids by accounting for the changes of the evaporation rate brought on by temperature and vapour accumulation around the source, which was done by coupling evaporation, temperature and dispersion modelling dynamically. Results show that DMC was significantly affected by the changes in liquid temperature during the evaporation, highlighting the importance on modelling the local energy balance of the evaporating solvent. The dispersion model showcased good qualitative agreement with the behaviour of heavy gasses, underlining that most of the dispersion of the vapours generated by DMC and DEC is buoyancy-based.

ce:section.ce:para: Climate change has become one of the most pressing challenges on a global scale due to its far-reaching consequences in our day-to-day life. There has been a worldwide effort to diminish the effects of climate change by scaling up the manufacturing and deployment of cost-competitive low-carbon technologies (Krishnan et al., 2023). To this end, batteries have become a key enabling technology to aid the green transition in important sectors, like mobility and energy storage. While still a growing industry, lithium ion batteries (LIB) are the most widespread among the rechargeable battery market (IEA et al., 2025).

Despite much effort to ensure battery safety, the increased reporting of incidents involving LIBs has triggered massive work to identify possible failure mechanisms (Reporter, 2025; Quraishi and Novak, 2025). Typical reasons for failure are found to be related to overheating of the battery through ageing (Ren et al., 2019), mechanical abuse (Xiao et al., 2023), internal short circuiting (An et al., 2024), manufacturing defects (Chen et al., 2024), and electrolyte leakage, which might lead to battery deterioration (Wang et al., 2022; Hu et al., 2023) and short circuiting (Liu et al., 2024). Many of the aforementioned failure modes may lead to dangerous vapour cloud emissions of hazardous compounds through the venting of the battery cell (Kalaikkanal and Gobinath, 2025). When not actively decomposing and catching fire, chemicals used in battery cells can be a serious environmental and human health hazard. Amongst these, the electrolyte, as one of the main components of a battery cell, poses a significant risk as a flammable, toxic and corrosive hazard.

These compounds are known to be highly volatile, flammable (Hess et al., 2015) and toxic (Lebedeva and Boon-Brett, 2016). In fact, because of its high toxicity, only 24.7 mL of dimethyl carbonate - a commonly used electrolyte solvent- is needed to create a potentially harmful atmosphere in a 61.5 m3 room (i.e., common size of garage with a medium size car and 0.5 m clearance underneath the vehicle), at room temperature (Lebedeva and Boon-Brett, 2016). Furthermore, large format prismatic Li-ion battery cells have been shown to contain a significant amount of free electrolyte, up to 35 mL (Lebedeva et al., 2019; Ferrario et al., 2024).

To ensure public safety, the United Nations’ Global Technical Regulation on Electric Vehicle Safety lists electrolyte leakage as an acceptance criterion for safety testing of batteries for electric vehicles. It stipulates the following requirements: “The general performance criteria for the tests of the REESS or vehicle controls is that during the test and for 1 h observation period after the test, there is no evidence of electrolyte leakage, rupture, venting, fire, or explosion, and electrical isolation of the REESS remains greater than 100 Ω/V.” (EVS et al., 2018).

Within this context, a leakage of a battery cell can result in a pool of liquid electrolyte, which would evaporate depending on the conditions of the environment it is released to. An accidental electrolyte leakage from a battery pack can create a dangerous atmosphere in poorly ventilated and/or enclosed environment, and electrolyte leakage being one of the most common battery failure modes (Wang et al., 2022) during operation, or even transportation (Maddipatla et al., 2024), highlighting the need for quick and reliable leak detection systems.

Crucially, while all battery failure modes mentioned lead to the generation of gasses and vapours which are highly toxic to humans when exposed, identification of a violent event like thermal runaway reactions is much simpler than an electrolyte leakage. Generally, the decomposition of battery cell materials will also be accompanied by smoke, sparks, and often, even jet fires (Bugryniec et al., 2024). On the other hand, electrolyte leakage manifests as the pooling of electrolyte which quickly vaporises due to its high volatility, making visual identification difficult. Hildebrand et al. (2024) compared various off-the-shelf solutions for the detection of vapours that are commonly generated by electrolyte solvents, concluding that currently there is no readily available method for specific and quantitative electrolyte vapour detection. While there are many promising qualitative gas analysis techniques (Sheng et al., 2025; Du et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2023) that could fit the needs for monitoring and early warning systems in enclosed environments, further development is needed. To design an appropriate detection system, a thorough understanding of the dynamics of cloud formation and dispersion of the volatile solvents is necessary. The focus of pure electrolyte solvents is because they are the most volatile components of the solution. Furthermore, Hess et al. (2015) showed that the presence of the solute has little influence on the evaporation of the electrolyte solution.

The computational fluid dynamics (CFD) approach has become common practice within industry to evaluate the behaviour of dispersing gasses within a multitude of accidental scenarios. In this work, a novel vaporisation model of free electrolyte solvents was designed through a CFD approach, by employing the Finite Volume Method (FVM). The presented model attempted to iterate on similar models that simulate the evaporation of liquids (Blázquez et al., 2017; Foncubierta Blázquez et al., 2018; Raimundo et al., 2014) through the implementation of a dynamically coupled temperature and dispersion evaporation model. This approach allows to assess the immediate effect of temperature changes of the source, along with concentration changes in the vapours in the surrounding environment. The approach was experimentally validated by measuring the evaporation rate and temperature changes of electrolyte solvents in a fully enclosed, quiescent environment, to mimic the evaporation conditions after an electrolyte leakage, e.g., from an EV, in a such an environment. This was the first step into building a model that will help gain insights into the toxic cloud dispersion processes, which would allow for the design of early warning systems, in case of accidental electrolyte release from Li-ion batteries.

The controlled evaporation experiments were performed in an aluminium box with a volume of 1 m3 (1 m x 1 m x1m) (see Fig. 1A). Aluminium was chosen as a material resistant to electrolyte solvents. The box was fitted with 5 nozzles that inject pressurised dry air to purge the contents of the box into an extraction point at the end of each experiment.

A technical balance (measurement error of ± 2.6 mg) inside the box was used to track the weight changes of the sample during the evaporation experiment. For monitoring this, an action camera and a light source were also added to the sample chamber. Temperature changes were measured during the experiment using a PT100 thermocouple submerged into the evaporating liquid. All measurements were performed in an enclosed, quiescent environment.

The evaporation experiments were performed with dimethyl carbonate (DMC, ≥99 %, Sigma-Aldrich) and diethyl carbonate (DEC, ≥99 %, Sigma-Aldrich) for the electrolyte solvents, and isopropyl alcohol (IPA, ≥99.8 %, Supelco) as the model compound. Solvents were added into a Petri dish with an internal diameter of 10 cm (area of 78.54 cm2) and an external diameter of 10.2 cm, with a height of 1 cm, placed on the balance. The solvent was added by injection through a PVC tube. After injection, the tube was slowly removed, and the box was completely sealed to avoid further generation of turbulence, which could affect the evaporation. Note that additional turbulence was added to the box after sealing during the injection of the solvent, where air was pushed in for circa 5 s before the liquid reached the Petri dish. To ensure that the Petri dish surface was fully coated with the liquid throughout the duration of the experiment, a total of 12 mL of compound was injected in each experimental run. Each experiment was performed at least three times.
Before the injection of the tested liquid, the box was partially sealed with an action camera and a light source to record the weight change of the balance.

The box presented an average temperature gradient from the base to the top of the box of 0.79 ± 0.09 °C, which was recorded for every experimental run with a pair of PT100 thermocouples at a height of 68 and 99 cm from the base of the box. Relative humidity was recorded at the beginning of each experiment. Tests were performed at room temperatures ranging from 17°C to 19.6°C. Relative humidity was measured to be between 30–65 % at the beginning of each experiment, before the box was closed.

The software employed to perform the numerical simulations was Fluent from Ansys® Workbench 2021R2, while the post processing was done with Python version 3.12.0. The CFD approach solves a system of linear conservation equations to simulate physical phenomena of momentum, energy and mass transport in a discretised computational domain.

The dispersion of vapours within a quiescent, confined environment, was simulated in this study. Authors that have previously simulated similar vapour or gas releases have agreed that the Reynolds Averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) model, which is an industry standard for most cases, is often sufficient to achieve acceptable results; in particular when using the Shear Stress Transport k-ω (SST k-ω) closure model for turbulence eddy modelling (Blázquez et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2020; Dong et al., 2017).

A major difference between the RANS model and more computationally expensive one, like a Large Eddy Simulation (LES) model, is the way eddies are simulated. For the RANS model, the closure equations model deals with the generation and dissipation of the eddies by calculating the turbulent kinetic energy (k) and the specific rate of dissipation (ω). Instead, the LES model fully simulates the generation and dissipation of eddies through the mesh, until a certain eddy size has been reached, at which point a Subgrid Scale (SGS) model is applied.

For this work, both RANS and LES models were tested:
•
The SST k-ω model with enabled Low Reynolds equations was used for RANS simulations.
•
The Wall-Adapting Local Eddy (WALE) viscosity model was used for LES simulations. The WALE model has shown good performance for wall-bounded flows, while spending up to 25 % less CPU time than other available models, such as the Dynamic Smagorinsky model (Ben-Nasr et al., 2017).

During the designing phase, a significant difference in temperature gradient definition between models was found. The RANS model has a tendency of inaccurately defining the small temperature gradient with which the simulation is initialised, estimating an average temperature for the whole domain. Although variations in temperature along the Y-axis of the box commonly remained below 1 °C during individual runs, they still have a noticeable effect on the evaporation rate. For this reason, the LES model was used for all simulations performed in this work.
More details on the governing equations and temperature gradient testing can be found in the Supplementary Material of this work.

The process of evaporation is a complex phenomenon that is very sensitive to the environment and geometry of the source. For environments without an induced flow, the process of evaporation can be described by a two-fold contribution:
•
Diffusion-based evaporation (also known as diffusion-limited evaporation (Stauber et al., 2014)), in which the diffusion of vapour is the driving force and limiting mechanism of the evaporation.
•
Natural convection-based evaporation, in which the convective flow drives unsaturated air towards the material boundary layer, which promotes the evaporation of the liquid (Perry and Green, 2008).

Some evaporation equations that attempt to capture the phenomenon use composite Sherwood numbers that include both contributions, but most of them were estimated whilst having an induced flow within the evaporation vessel (Poós and Varju, 2024). Equations designed in such a manner so that when convection-based evaporation ceases (e.g., in the case of a vapour-saturated environment), diffusion-limited evaporation becomes dominant.

Such models seldom account for the effect of the temperature drop caused by the evaporation process, and the impact of an increasing concentration gradient close to the liquid-vapour interface. The driving force for convective heat transfer and convective evaporation is accounted for by estimating the difference between the concentration and temperature at the interface and at a bulk distance. This bulk distance is generally assumed to be infinitely removed. This assumption is not without merit for large environments that have a significant induced flow where vapour accumulation does not occur. But for a relatively small domain like the one tested for this work, this assumption cannot be made. As such, through the CFD approach used in this work, it was possible to couple the evaporation and temperature decrease of the simulated solvent, with the concentration of solvent and temperature changes at a bulk distance, thus accounting for the dispersion rate of the vapours in the computational domain.

The chosen bulk distance for this work was the characteristic length of the Petri dish, i.e., its radius of 5 cm. In fact, for diffusion-based evaporation problems, where induced flow does not contribute to the evaporation of the liquid, the characteristic dimension of the evaporating source has been used as the bulk distance (Bird et al., 2007). This is because the characteristic length is a minimum reasonable distance at which the accumulated vapours may have an effect in the evaporation process. More details on the choice of the bulk distance can be found in the Supplementary Material.

Dollet et al. (2017) formulated an equation that would account for the change in size of the material boundary layer adjacent to the liquid-vapour interface. The leading assumption is that a flat disk can be divided into three zones, so that it is possible to quantify how each zone contributes to the overall evaporation rate at a given temperature (see Fig. 2).
The direction of the evaporation rate and spatial structure of the boundary layer are constructed by assuming a positive density difference between saturated and unsaturated air.

The quantification of the material boundary layer thickness was done with the same rescaling variables used for the thermal boundary layer by Stewartson (Stewartson, 1958), which allowed Dollet et al. to design a set of equations to express the evaporation rate contribution of each zone. Since the centre zone represents a very small portion of the disk, it is neglected and treated with the same equation as the intermediate zone. The equations for intermediate and edge zones are the following (Dollet and Boulogne, 2017):

The contributions of each evaporation zone are weighted depending on the position of each cell relative to the origin of the frame of reference. The origin in the computational domain is positioned at X0 (0,0,0), as shown is Fig. 3. As such, the position of the cell is measured by accounting for the radial distance from the centre of the Petri dish by using the following formula:

While research of Dollet et al. suggests a specific distance of transition from one equation and the other, this change is equivalent to using a step function at a critical value. This leads to convergence difficulties with the numerical model of this work. Li et al. (2022) faced a similar problem when using a numerical approach to model the decomposition of electrolyte components due to a thermal runaway reaction in a battery pack. The heat released by the decomposition sharply changed at critical temperatures, creating simulation instabilities, which lead to converges difficulties for the Finite Element Method model (Li et al., 2022). The approach of applying a smoothing function that describes the transition from one equation to the other, with the width and centre of transition estimated with experimental data, yielded a more consistent solution. As such, the solution proposed by Li et al. was adopted for this work. The smoothing function and a final evaporation rate equation applied to the evaporation source term for the Dollet approach were:

These parameters were estimated by performing a linear regression fitting on the simulation results and experimental evaporation data of DMC and DEC. More details can be found in the Supplementary Material. All simulations performed with the Dollet approach used Eq. (6) as the evaporation rate.

Few correlations have been published for natural convection evaporation in a quiescent environment, which are mostly developed for water evaporation (Poós and Varju, 2024; Bower and Saylor, 2009). Most of these are used for the evaporation rate of stagnant pools of water in an indoor environment, which unfortunately do not match the dynamics of the vapour flow expected, as the geometry of this work is several orders of magnitude smaller. Recognising this gap in literature, Kelly-Zion et al. (2013) developed a correlation formulated for sessile droplets for various volatile hydrocarbons. By using an approach similar to Bower and Saylor (2009), Kelly-Zion et al. created a Rayleigh-based Sherwood number relation, and broke it into various parts to allow each dimensionless section to influence the equation to a different degree by having individual exponents. In addition, the diffusion-limited contribution was added to the natural convection contribution in the form of a sum of Sherwood numbers.

Thus, Kelly-Zion’s correlation was defined as follows (Kelly-Zion et al., 2013):

ce:section.ce:section-title: Coupled temperature model – local heat balance
ce:section.ce:para:
Other works that have performed convection evaporation numerical simulations (Blázquez et al., 2017; Foncubierta Blázquez et al., 2018; Raimundo et al., 2014; Vinnichenko et al., 2019) have suggested the use of a fixed temperature for the source, expecting a minimal temperature change of the liquid phase at the considered evaporation rates. Nevertheless, during the experimental tests of this work, it was possible to measure a noticeable effect of the temperature drop on the evaporation rate during the experimental tests of this work. For this reason, a local heat balance for the liquid surface, as previously done by Vinnichenko (Vinnichenko et al., 2013), was applied:

To estimate the heat transfer coefficients in Eq. (10), between the surface of the liquid and the vapour phase, the following correlations were employed (Bird et al., 2007; Goldstein et al., 1973):

, α is the thermal diffusion coefficient. Eq. (12) is a well-known equation by Goldstein et al. (1973) developed for free convection above a horizontal flat surface. This equation is valid between Ra values of 2 × 102–104.

The composition of the flow is as important as the amount of material entering the domain, as it is a major driving force for buoyant and diffusive dispersion. In Blázquez et al. (2017) and Foncubierta Blázquez et al. (2018) simulations, the material boundary layer above the liquid-vapour interface of the water surface was considered to be fully saturated. Vapour losses in this layer, which occur through diffusion and advection, are compensated by the evaporation of the liquid phase. Through this condition, Blázquez et al. were able to justify the use of a multicomponent, gaseous mono-phase simulation approach. The liquid phase was not simulated, but the saturated gas cloud that serves as a de-facto inlet of vapours of the evaporated liquid and defines inlet concentration.

This approach was also adopted by this work, with two differences:
•
The boundary condition in this work was defined through a mass flowrate Q . This was assumed to be constant throughout the material boundary layer.
•
The condition of a saturated material boundary layer was not used. It was assumed that the boundary layer dissipates from the interface to the edge of the boundary layer in accordance to the mass-transfer theory of diffusion in a stagnant film (Perry and Green, 2008).

The mass-transfer theory is valid for the unidirectional diffusion in an ideal system of the component through a film of gas (i.e., air) (Bird et al., 2007):

Eq. (16) was used to define the concentration inlet for every simulation performed in this work. The advantage of this method is that the dissipation of the vapours of the evaporated liquid that occurs in the material boundary layer does not need to be simulated, it is instead modelled (see Fig. 4). In practice, this approach introduces the vapour cloud in the simulated domain, without the need to simulate the material boundary layer dissipation.

To fully simulate the evaporation process of all tested compounds, several thermophysical parameters for both liquid and gaseous phases of the tested compounds need to be known. Table 1 contains a list of parameters used for the models.

Through evaporation experiments, Seo and Wang (2021) demonstrated that there is a linear dependency on the vapour pressure and the binary diffusion coefficient of electrolyte solvents like DMC. Seo et al.’s work demonstrated that the binary diffusion coefficient equals to 1.97 × 10−5 m2/s at 21°C for DMC with a vapour pressure of 2400 Pa. Currently, various sources indicate the vapour pressure of DMC is much higher, at around 6050 Pa at 21°C (Zhou et al., 2011; Verevkin et al., 2008).

Using the low vapour pressure as suggested by Seo et al., or the a high vapour pressure as reported elsewhere (Zhou et al., 2011; Verevkin et al., 2008), would have a strong effect on the modelling of the liquid-vapour interface of DMC. Consequently, both approaches were used and tested to compare their results against experimental data:
•
Simulations of DMC that use the low vapour pressure value were all performed with a vapour pressure of 2400 Pa and with a binary diffusion coefficient of 1.97 × 10−5 m2/s, as reported by Seo et al.
•
Simulations of DMC that use the high vapour pressure value were all performed by using the equation reported in Table 1, estimated using a semi-empirical Antoine equation. Moreover, since the binary diffusion coefficient estimated by Seo et al. requires the use of a low vapour pressure, Marrero and Mason’s correlation (see Table 2) was used for this approach. The binary diffusion coefficient was estimated to be 9.42 × 10−6 m2/s at 21°C.

The geometry of the computational domain is shown in Fig. 5. Multiple objects present in the experimental box were included in the model to account for possible interactions with the vapours. For the most part, these elements were found to have little effect on dispersion, due to their relatively small volume when compared to the computational domain. They were nevertheless included in the simulations to describe the set-up more accurately.

The origin of the domain X
0 is at coordinates (0,0,0), which was used as a reference point for all other geometries. Distance d is the value used in Section 2.3.1 to account for the spatial positioning of centre of the Petri dish for the Dollet evaporation approach. Characteristics of the various geometry volumes are listed in Table 3.

Height from the base of the domain and the object is H
obj,b = 0.525 m, and the diameter is D
obj = 0.15 m. The bulk distance described in Section 2.3 equals to the height of the balance Hb, the height of the Petri dish and the radius of the evaporating source, for a total of 12.7 cm from the base of the box, positioned directly above the Petri dish.

A polyhedral mesh was utilised since each cell possess a high number of faces neighbouring with other cells, which in turn allows for better gradient computation. Due to the high dependency of the dispersion of simulated vapours for the results of the evaporation model, a large number of cells was necessary. Mesh parameters are reported in Table 4. With a nominal cell size of 5 mm, for a total of around 3.04 million cells. By positioning each object in the experimental tests in such a way that the domain could be split into two symmetrical halves, a symmetry plane methodology could be applied, halving the total number of cells to 1.52 million. Cells adjacent to the inlet boundary layer were defined through an inflation layer method, as to ensure that a well-developed gradient is simulated. With a total thickness of 10 mm and a growth rate of 1.1, this space was divided into 18 layers, as shown in Fig. 5. The smallest layer is 0.4 mm thick.

A mesh and time step independence were performed with the RANS model, which was then used as a baseline for a resolution quality test for the LES model. More details can be found in the Supplementary Material to this work.

Before initialising the simulation, it is necessary to define the boundary conditions for each surface of the model. Common practice in simulations employing the RANS models is to include the roughness height of walls, to model the effects of viscosity on the wall-bounded flows (sub-viscous laminar layers) when the mesh is too coarse to resolve the flow (Ansys,Inc, 2021). On the other hand, LES models do not enable the inclusion of roughness height, as no velocity dampening is applied to the flow, rather the wall effects are fully simulated (Ansys,Inc, 2021). For this reason, the roughness height was not considered for all simulations. Thus, all walls were only defined with a no-slip condition.

Species transport was enabled with multicomponent diffusion and inlet diffusion. Dry air and water vapour were used to include the effects of changing relative humidity values in the experiments; thus, the binary diffusion coefficient of air-water vapour was estimated with Chung’s equation in Table 2, yielding a 5.9 % difference between its estimated value at 20°C and experimental data. The same equation was used for binary diffusion coefficient between the simulated compound and water vapour. The incompressible ideal gas density models were used for this work.
There is a single mass flow inlet boundary condition within the model from the Petri dish. All the properties of the flow were included in the form of a User Defined Function (UDF).

During initialisation, the mass flow rate was defined with an initial inlet temperature to calculate the vapour pressure of the compound, as related in Table 2. With these, as shown in Fig. 6, the inlet concentration term was estimated by using Eq. (16). The mass flow rate calculated with Eqs. (6) or (8), depending on the model, was then updated and its results used to update the temperature profile at the inlet with Eq. (9). Finally, the material flowrate, temperature and concentration were utilised as the mass flow rate input for the domain. The UDF script updated the bulk matrix used for the temperature and concentration recorded at the bulk distance indicated in Section 2.3 each time step.

After initialisation, a temperature profile and water vapour content were patched depending on the temperature gradient from the base to the top of the box, and measured relative humidity as indicated in Table 5. Three experimental scenarios were simulated. Each scenario was repeated for each evaporation model (with an additional scenario for DMC due to the low/high vapour cases referenced in Section 2.4) for a total of 8 simulations.

All simulations were performed with the pressure-velocity coupled solver, with bounded central differencing for the momentum equation and the second upwind scheme for all other linear conservation equations. As convergence criterion, default Fluent settings were kept for most residual convergence parameters, except for the continuity equation, which was lowered from 10−3 to 10−4 for DMC simulations. Generally, the lower the flowrate, the more difficult it is to obtain convergence on each timestep. To initialise the simulation, a flow of air was introduced in the domain to reduce computational instabilities for both evaporation approaches with a 0.05 s timestep, and to replicate the air flow experimentally introduced in the box during injection (see Section 2.1). After 5 simulated seconds, timestep values were changed and the solvent vapour was also introduced. After low residuals and satisfactory simulation stability were reached, the UDF script was enabled, with 1 s timesteps for IPA simulations, while all DMC and DEC simulations were performed with 0.5 s timesteps to ensure that convergence criteria were reached. More information related to the timestep independence can be found in the Supplementary Material. Simulations ran for 2400 s, with an average CPU time per timestep of 10 s.

To evaluate the numerical model performance, a statistical analysis based on the measure of the Geometric variance (GV) and Geometric mean bias (GM), recommended by Hanna to assess gas dispersion models, and the fraction of the factor of two (FAC2) was used (Hanna et al., 1993):

The value of GV measures the scatter of the model compared to experimental data, while GM is the ratio of the geometric mean between observed and predicted points. When plotting the GV and GM value, it is often useful to indicate the inherent minimum variance that any model has at a given GM value:

A model is considered good when GV < 1.6, 0.7 <GM< 1.3 and the FAC2 value is within the ranges of Eq. (19). Furthermore, results with GM value < 1 indicate that the model overpredicts experimental data, while values > 1 indicate that the model underpredicts experimental data.

Measured and simulated evaporation fluxes of all three compounds in a temperature range between 17°C to 19.6°C are presented in
Figs. 7, 8 and 9 for IPA, DMC and DEC, respectively.
Fig. 10 shows the comparison between experimental and simulated temperature change during the evaporation experiments for IPA, DMC and DEC.

Throughout the process of evaporation, the evaporation fluxes were consistent among various experiments. IPA and DMC had an average evaporation flux of 0.36 and 0.70 mg/(cm2 min), respectively. After an initial drop, a clear downward trend was recorded for both compounds (See Figs. 7 A and 8A). This trend is not representative for DEC, as the evaporation flux seem to increase along the duration of the experiment (see Fig. 9 A). After 540 s, the evaporation flux had stabilised to an average of 0.15 mg/(cm2 min), while still showing significant stochastic variations as low as 0.14 mg/(cm2 min) (at 1080 s) and as high as 0.17 mg/(cm2 min) (at 2400 s). In addition, the measurement error of the balance is 2.6 mg, which in the context of the average evaporation rate of DEC (i.e., 12.1 mg/ min), it is equivalent to an error of ca. 21.5 %.

As mentioned in Section 2.3.3, temperature changes had a measurable effect on the evaporation flux. For DMC a temperature reduction of about 0.7°C occurs in 120 s, which is slightly more than IPA at 0.54°C, and more than ten times of DEC at < 0.1°C, which is linked to the evaporation flux drop measured in that same period. This is because of the high evaporation flux of DMC, which is twice as high as IPA and five times as fast as DEC in a similar temperature range. Beyond the initial temperature decrease, the average total temperature decrease during the experiment of IPA and DMC is comparable at 1.2 °C and 1.3°C, respectively. IPA has a smooth temperature curve, lacking the sharp dip in temperature, which was recorded with DMC at the beginning of the experiment. The curves are comparable, which could be caused by the mirrored relationship between their evaporation flux and the heat of evaporation between IPA and DMC, as listed in Table 1. The evaporation flux of IPA is about half of that of DMC, and its heat of evaporation is almost twice as high as that of DMC. While DEC shows a definite decrease in temperature, it is seemingly restrained by the low evaporation flux. It is evident that at the beginning of the experiment, the liquid DEC had a period of a stable temperature, possibly due to the low vaporisation heat being compensated by the environment. Nevertheless, after around 540 s, a moderate reduction and plateau of temperature was observed, with a total decline of only 0.4°C.

The FAC2 statistical analysis for Dollet and Kelly-Zion approaches give acceptable results for the prediction of the evaporation flux (estimated as the ratio of the evaporation rate and the surface area) of almost all IPA, DMC and DEC scenarios (see Figs. 7B, 8B and 9B).

Fig. 11 shows a clear trend in the behaviour of the proposed methods to estimate the evaporation flux of the tested solvents. For VG, no additional variance was present, as no stochastic variation can be justified by the CFD model, thus the minimum value at a given GM value (see Eq. (20)) was found. For GM, it is evident that the Dollet approach has a tendency to underpredict the experimental data, while the Kelly-Zion approach has a tendency to overpredict it.

The only case in which results exit the range of acceptability is for the evaporation of DMC with the high vapour pressure approach, which heavily overpredicts the observed values with the Kelly-Zion approach, while still giving good predictions with the Dollet approach. For low vapour pressure simulations of DMC, both the Dollet and Kelly-Zion approach are within the range of acceptability. It is clear that the Kelly-Zion approach is not useable for simulations that use the high vapour pressure value for DMC.

The difference between Dollet and Kelly-Zion’s equations is related to the way the vapour pressure and diffusion coefficient are accounted for in each version of the DMC simulations. While the Dollet approach assumes a linear relationship between the vapour pressure and the diffusion coefficient (Eqs. (1), (2) and (6)), the Kelly-Zion approach is based on an inverse logarithmic relationship between vapour pressure and atmospheric pressure (Eq. (7) and an exponential dependence of the diffusion coefficient (Eq. (8)). Thus, a change of vapour pressure or diffusion coefficient can easily be compensated for in the Dollet’s equations, but not in the Kelly Zion’s.

Furthermore, the tendency to overpredict by the Kelly-Zion approach might be because the compounds for which Eq. (8) had been formulated are chemically very different from the ones used in this work. In fact, acetone and methanol are the only oxygenated compounds used by Kelly-Zion et al. for the calibration of the evaporation correlation. As a way of example, while the diffusivity coefficient of acetone and methanol are similar to those of IPA and DMC with low vapour pressure, the vapour pressure values of the former two are significantly higher than the latter two at room temperature. DMC with the high vapour pressure approach has properties that might fall out of the range of applicability of Eq. (8). This demonstrates an incompatibility in the expected relationship between the vapour pressure and the diffusion coefficient predicted in Kelly-Zion et al.’s work, when compared to the compounds tested in this work.
Nevertheless, both the Dollet and Kelly-Zion approach demonstrated results that allowed them to be considered “good models” for most of the simulations presented, as defined in Section 2.6.

The temperature model used to predict the temperature drop have a unitary FAC2, as the discrepancies of the predicted values are small when compared to experimental observations. While acceptable by the metrics imposed in Section 2.6, the temperature model for every approach tends to plateau at fixed values.

Fig. 12 indicates that the model is accurately predicting the change in temperature in the most important part of the experiment, i.e., the beginning, where sharp temperature changes have the most significant effect on the evaporation. The plateau that was reached by each scenario showcases an overcompensation of heat after the sharp decrease and stabilisation of temperature after the 500 s of simulated time. It was shown that the closer the model is at predicting the evaporation flux, the higher the accuracy of the temperature decrease during the initial period of simulated time. The only cases in which the temperature drop is overestimated was by DMC (with the high vapour pressure value) and IPA simulations with the Kelly-Zion approach, due to their evaporation flux predictions, which are significantly higher than experimental values.

Although all presented models tended to a quasi-stable temperature, the period of time in which the major temperature drop occurred was consistent with experimental data for all cases. Improvements to the model could be related to the underlying assumptions made the for temperature model. The temperature was assumed to be uniform in the liquid phase due to the small thickness of the pool, such that modelling of the liquid was not necessary. Furthermore, since a uniform temperature distribution in the Petri dish was assumed, the heat exchange between the liquid and solid phase were not simulated. As such the inclusion of the temperature exchanged at the base of the Petri dish was not included in the energy balance equation. Simulating the full system could more accurately depict the temperature drop experimentally recorded. Moreover, a better control of experimental parameters, e.g. temperature (changes) of the volume evaporation takes place into could decrease the experimental error too. Currently, only DMC with the high vapour pressure approach (Dollet approach) managed to follow the temperature drop trend that was experimentally measured with good accuracy, while at the same time matching the evaporation flux for all 2400 simulated seconds. Even so, this work shows that using only the initial conditions of the evaporation process can still produce reliable results with the present models, whilst showing an accurate representation of the most significant temperature and evaporation flux drops.

An additional important aspect to the evaporation model, was the ability of the numerical method to simulate the dispersion of the vapours injected into the computational domain.
Figs. 13 and 14 show that the compounds that were simulated for this work have the typical behaviour of negative buoyancy jets (often referred as fountain flow) (Hunt and Burridge, 2015).

With all compounds being heavier than air, there is a propensity to accumulate at the bottom of the box, although this happens at different speeds depending on the compound. Certainly, compounds with high vapour pressure and diffusion coefficient, and low molar weight have faster dispersion speeds. IPA and DMC with the high vapour pressure approach show similar results, although DMC is faster (see Fig. 13). While the vapour pressure of IPA is only 73.7 % of the vapour pressure of DMC with the high vapour pressure approach, DMC has a molar weight that is 50 % higher than that of IPA. With similar dynamic viscosity and diffusion coefficients, the difference in vapour pressure and molar weight is the reason for their similar behaviour. DMC with the low vapour pressure approach and DEC have lower vapour pressures such that their dispersion rate is limited, as shown in Fig. 14. Furthermore, DEC is the heavier compound, with the lowest diffusion coefficient that was simulated in this work.

While the effect of the dispersion rate on the evaporation model is not immediately evident, after 540 s, it becomes dominant. When sharp temperature drops stop and the heat of evaporation is mostly compensated by the environment while it slowly plateaus (see Figs. 7 A, 8A, 9A and 10), the evaporation flux of IPA and DMC start decreasing at a quasi-constant rate. There is a reduction of the evaporation flux due to the decreasing contribution of natural convection described in Section 2.3, as the vapours accumulate around the source. This effect was modelled by accounting for the changing value of the bulk concentration measured at the bulk distance above the Petri dish, as noted in equations from Sections 2.3.1 and 2.3.2.

The accuracy of prediction of the decrease rate of the evaporation flux will change depending on the compound and model used. This change was estimated after 540 s, which is around the time at which temperature decrease ceased:
•
For IPA, there was an average decrease in evaporation flux of 1.06 × 10−3 mg/(cm2 min2), with the Dollet and Kelly-Zion approaches underpredicting the decrease by 52 % and 75 %, respectively.
•
For DMC-Low vapour pressure approach, there was an average decrease in evaporation flux of 2.79 × 10−3 mg/(cm2 min2), with the Dollet and Kelly-Zion approaches underpredicting the decrease by 45.2 % and 83 %, respectively.
•
For DMC-High vapour pressure approach, there was an average decrease in evaporation flux of 2.79 × 10−3 mg/(cm2 min2), with the Dollet and Kelly-Zion approaches underpredicting the decrease by 44 % and 61 %, respectively.
This analysis was not possible for DEC, due to smaller evaporation flux variations which are smaller than the systematic error of the balance.

From Eq. (16), a higher evaporation flow flux signifies larger dissipation within the material boundary layer. This leads to less material being introduced in the computational model. Thus, the Kelly-Zion approach always underperformed when compared to the Dollet approach. A more in-depth analysis on the effectiveness of the dispersion rate of the Kelly-Zion and Dollet approaches has been carried out, where the experimental and simulated dispersion data are compared (Ferrario et al., 2025). Videos of the first 1200 s of the dispersion of DMC with the high vapour pressure approach and DEC can be found within the Supplementary Material.

To have better insights into the dynamics of the evaporation and vapour cloud formation for Li-ion battery electrolyte solvents, which are necessary for the design of early warning systems in enclosed environments, a combined experimental and computational fluid dynamics simulations approach has been used. Experimental data generated during evaporation of pure DMC and DEC as well as IPA as a model compound with well-documented thermophysical properties was used to validate two evaporation models proposed for the CFD simulations of the studied components.

The average quasi-steady-state evaporation flux measured for DMC, DEC and IPA were 0.70, 0.15 and 0.36 mg/(cm2 min), respectively. It was demonstrated that the temperature drop had a measurable effect on the evaporation flux of DMC and IPA, up until convective effects become more relevant after the temperature of the liquid phase had plateaued.

By using evaporation equations from literature, it was possible to design a novel approach for the source term modelling of natural convection evaporation, along with the dispersion of the generated vapours. All simulations presented were done with the LES model due to its greater ability to define small temperature gradients compared to the RANS model.

Two approaches to modelling the source term were presented: the Dollet and Kelly-Zion approach. This led to the following results:
•
The Dollet approach consistently outperformed the Kelly-Zion approach by more reliably predicting the evaporation rate of all simulated compounds.
•
With the Dollet approach, the local heat balance model managed to predict the temperature drop of all tested compounds, capturing the most significant temperature changes at the beginning of the simulation.
•
The radius of the evaporating source was shown to be a reasonable bulk distance from which bulk values can be estimated
Overall, the coupling of the evaporation and the temperature model, along with the dispersion method proved to be capable of replicating the evaporation rate that was experimentally measured.

Due to the versatility of the CFD method, the proposed model can be of relevance for other release scenarios such as leakage during the storage and transportation (Jeevarajan et al., 2022) and/or recycling of batteries (Xu et al., 2023), where environments with poor ventilation may lead to the formation of toxic and flammable atmospheres. It is important to note that the Dollet model was specifically designed for natural convection evaporation, i.e., for quiescent environments. Future work would need to focus on possible extension of the applicability of the approach for forced convection evaporation, which is typical for open field, windy scenarios. This would lead to the formulation of an evaporation equation that depends on a Sherwood number defined to include the Reynolds number.

This work has been further developed by applying the CFD model for advanced studies of the vapour cloud dispersion in an enclosed environment, while comparing it with experimental results (Ferrario et al., 2025), which provides engineers with the necessary insight on the behaviour of the cloud, to deal with vapour build up in the most appropriate manner.
